text: The surgeon carefully unwraps the wristed instruments, which are ready for use straight out of the box without requiring any setup.

The surgeon inserts the camera into the chest cavity, revealing the diaphragm positioned high. The gas-powered camera holder allows for easy adjustment with a button press, maintaining its position for a stable view.

The surgeon tests the movement capabilities of the needle holder in the left hand and a Cadier instrument in the right hand, demonstrating the left, right, up, and down motions without wrist attachments.
The surgeon tests the diaphragm to determine the optimal locations for placation stitches, using Tuo Ethibond sutures without pledgets.

The surgeon places the first horizontal mattress suture in the center of the diaphragm, aligning it for future sutures. The diaphragm appears bowed towards the surgeon, and CO₂ pressure is increased to facilitate the procedure.

The surgeon places a horizontal mattress suture at the back of the tendinous portion of the diaphragm using wristed instruments, demonstrating their versatility in achieving precise angles and directions for needle placement and extraction.
The surgeon adjusts the direction of the needle using a Cadi Air instrument, which provides a gentle grip, while the needle holder offers a stronger grip strength for precise manipulation.
The surgeon places the knot on the anterior portion using a Chitwood knot pusher, facilitating quick and efficient knot tying.

The surgeon performs a second horizontal mattress stitch using a backhand technique, pulling the suture through with the Cadi Air instrument. The needle holder is used to grasp the diaphragm tissue securely.

The surgeon places a stitch on the diaphragm, avoiding grabbing lung tissue with the needle holder, while using wristed instruments similar to robotic techniques. The needle is positioned at ninety degrees in the needle holder for precise suturing.

The surgeon uses a right-handed instrument to grasp and create a ruffle in the diaphragm, ensuring no spleen or stomach is behind it. A nasogastric tube is visible on the left side for stomach decompression. The surgeon ties a Western knot, effectively controlling any minor bleeding from the diaphragm's rich vascular supply.

The surgeon performs instrument ties using wristed instruments, demonstrating ease in knot tying due to the device's design. Twelve horizontal mattress sutures are placed to tighten the diaphragm, with visible pericardium in the bottom right of the view. The surgeon ensures no structures are behind the diaphragm while suturing, using a Cavidian EndoStitch for challenging areas in the posterolateral corner.

The surgeon uses wristed instruments to press on the diaphragm, assessing its tightness without CO₂ insufflation. A Maryland bipolar instrument is employed to unroof a cyst located in the mediastinal fat near the phrenic nerve, which is paralyzed. The cyst is visually confirmed not to be a thymoma, and a needle is used to aspirate and decompress it.
